title: Śrī Vīrastava (The Third Smaraṇa)
summary: Vīrastava is a short, jewel-like hymn of praise (stava) to Mahāvīra, the twenty-fourth and last Tīrthaṅkara, here recited as the third of the Nine Smaraṇas (nava-smaraṇa) — a set of protective remembrances chanted in the Pākṣika (fortnightly) pratikramaṇa. In just six Prakrit gāthās it salutes Mahāvīra by comparing his serene beauty to the loveliest things of nature — a fresh lotus, a majestic elephant, the full moon — and closes by asking that the Lord destroy all troubles and misfortunes. A newcomer can simply hear it as a devotional poem of praise and protection; the recurring images of lotus, elephant and moon carry its meaning.
ritualPlacement: Within the Pākṣika (Pakkhī) pratikramaṇa, the Vīrastava is the third of the Nine Smaraṇas (nava-smaraṇa), the fixed cluster of protective stotras recited after the two Nandī-sūtra sajjhāys and before the Pakkhī Khāmaṇā. Because Cāumāsī (Cāturmāsika) and Sāṁvatsarika have no independent sūtra text of their own — they reproduce the entire Pākṣika sequence with only the word-substitution (પક્ખી → ચઉમાસી / સંવત્સરિ) and the enlarged kāyotsarga (12 / 20 / 40 Logassa) — this same Vīrastava is recited, unchanged, in those two observances as well. It is therefore a shared, Pakkhī-anchored text and is never an independent Cāumāsī/Sāṁvatsarika composition.
authorAndSource: The authoritative book (Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit, Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.) prints the six gāthās but names no author for the Vīrastava ('—' in the Nine-Smaraṇa inventory). The hymn's own final gāthā states that Vīra is 'pālittaya-mayamahio' — greatly honoured by Pālitta(ya), i.e. Ācārya Pādaliptasūri (Pālittasūri); on this basis the tradition often associates the stava with Pādaliptasūri, but the book does not assert authorship and external attributions are not uniform, so this is left flagged in the notes rather than stated as fact.
structureArc: The six gāthās move from acclamation to invocation to portraiture to benediction. v.1 opens with the cry of victory ('jayai') and the governing simile-set: Vīra's petal-like eyes (lotus) and his graceful, elephant-king stride. v.2 widens the frame to time and place — even today his pure tīrtha (ford/teaching) flows unbroken in Bharata-kṣetra — and hails Vardhamānasvāmī as the sun of the three worlds. v.3 is the poet's own resolve: with this pair of gāthās ('gāhā-juala') he will praise ('thosāmi') the Jina who is free of pride (mada), delusion (moha) and the passions (kasāya), the utterly detached (nissaṅga) Mahāvīra. vv.4–5 are the heart of the poem: they develop the threefold nature-imagery (see recurringImagery) — soft/firm/gentle, the three colours, the ornaments of water, land and sky. v.6 is the closing maṅgala: thus praised by the host of apsarases and by the Saṅgha, and honoured by Pālitta, may Vīra-Jinendra grant the destruction of all calamities ('khayaṁ savva-duriyāṇaṁ').
recurringImagery: The hymn is built on a single triad of natural emblems, introduced in v.1 and elaborated across vv.4–5: the lotus (paṅkaja), the lordly elephant (gajendra) and the moon (candra). These are mapped onto Mahāvīra's person — the lotus to his wide, petal-like eyes; the elephant-king's measured majesty to his gait and bearing; the full moon to the calm radiance of his face. The three are further keyed to the three realms they naturally adorn: the lotus is the ornament of water (jala), the elephant of land (sthala), the moon of the sky (nabhas) — so the 'three' (tinni) ornaments of water, land and sky are all gathered into the one body of the Jina. The verses press the conceit by saying these natural beauties themselves cannot bear ('na cayanti') to relinquish Vīra's grace. Read vv.4–5 with this key and the dense compounds resolve into a single sustained portrait; the per-verse commentaries need only point to which limb each emblem adorns.
keyThemes: Praise (stava) of Mahāvīra, the last Tīrthaṅkara
Protective remembrance (smaraṇa) and removal of calamities
Beauty likened to lotus, elephant and moon (the three natural ornaments)
Freedom from pride, delusion and the passions (mada–moha–kaṣāya)
The unbroken tīrtha still flowing in Bharata-kṣetra
Mahāvīra as the sun of the three worlds

glossary.definition: A hymn of praise to a Jina; here, praise of Vīra (hence Vīra-stava).
A 'remembrance' — a protective stotra; Vīrastava is the third of the nine recited in the Pākṣika pratikramaṇa.
The twenty-fourth and final Tīrthaṅkara of this era, also called Vardhamāna; the subject of this hymn.
Mahāvīra's given name, meaning 'the increasing/ever-growing one' (Prakrit Vaddhamāṇa).
Literally a 'ford'; the Jina's order/teaching that ferries beings across saṁsāra — said in v.2 to flow on unbroken in Bharata-kṣetra.
Bharata, the continent/region of our world where Tīrthaṅkaras arise and the tīrtha endures.
A Prakrit verse in the āryā-type metre; the poet calls his praise a 'gāhā-juala' (pair of gāthās) in v.3.
Pride or intoxicating conceit, one of the faults Vīra has wholly transcended (v.3).
Delusion/deluding karma; its absence marks the Jina's perfect insight.
The four passions (anger, pride, deceit, greed); Vīra is 'jiya-kasāya', conqueror of the passions.
Unattached, free of all clinging; an epithet of the liberated Mahāvīra (v.3).
Lotus — emblem of Vīra's eyes and the ornament of water (jala).
Lord of elephants — emblem of Vīra's majestic gait and the ornament of land (sthala).
Moon — emblem of Vīra's serene face and the ornament of the sky (nabhas).
Celestial nymphs; in v.6 the 'accharagaṇa' (host of apsarases) join the Saṅgha in praising the Jina.
Calamity, misfortune, evil/sin; the closing prayer asks Vīra for the 'destruction of all duritas' (savva-duriyāṇaṁ khaya).
Ācārya Pālitta/Pādalipta, named in v.6 as one who greatly honoured Vīra; sometimes linked to the hymn's authorship (see notes).

howToRead: A new learner can read Vīrastava as a single sustained portrait of Mahāvīra — hold the three images of lotus (eyes), elephant (gait) and moon (face) in mind and the rest follows, ending in the prayer that he 'destroy all troubles'. An advanced practitioner can attend to the metrical 'gāhā-juala' structure, the precise mapping of the water/land/sky ornaments in vv.4–5, the doctrinal weight of jiya-kasāya / nissaṅga, and the self-referential homage to Pālitta in v.6.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim text of the six gāthās, placement of Vīrastava as the 3rd of the Nine Smaraṇas within Pakkhī, and the absence of a named author.

sources.title: Jaina Stotra Sandoha / Nava Smaraṇa collections (standard editions)
sources.consultedFor: The standard nava-smaraṇa ordering and the textual identity of the Vīrastava.

notes: The authoritative book names no author for the Vīrastava. The hymn's final gāthā describes Vīra as 'pālittaya-mayamahio' (greatly honoured by Pālitta = Ācārya Pādaliptasūri), and on this slender internal cue the tradition sometimes associates the stava with Pādaliptasūri; an alternative external attribution to Siddhasena Divākara also circulates. Both are uncertain and not corroborated by the source book, so authorship is best treated as undetermined. Per the book's orthography policy the Prakrit readings (mātrās, anusvāras, conjuncts) are authoritative as printed; transliterations of individual words here follow IAST conventions and are approximate to the book's Gujarati-script Prakrit. The triad-and-realm reading of vv.4–5 follows the natural sense of paṅkaya/gayanda/canda with jala/thala/naha; where a compound is ambiguous the per-verse commentary should flag it rather than over-resolve it.
